buyer_views.answer_contract: Return protocol profiles, current source references, conformance checks, generated evidence paths, and runtime decisions.

protocols.commercial_value: Turns MCP authorization drift into a premium gateway control: live metadata discovery, resource/audience checks, client metadata review, consent evidence, and signed runtime receipts.

conformance_checks.buyer_question: Can SecurityRecipes prove which MCP resource a token was minted for before a tool call is allowed?
conformance_checks.requirement: Protected MCP servers must expose and verify resource metadata before authorization decisions are trusted.

conformance_checks.requirement: Raw user or upstream API tokens must not be passed through an agent/tool path.
conformance_checks.title: Token Passthrough Denial

protocols.title: MCP Authorization Conformance

conformance_checks.buyer_question: What happens when a tool changes after a previous approval?
conformance_checks.requirement: Descriptions, input schemas, output schemas, annotations, and capability flags must be pinned and re-reviewed on drift.
conformance_checks.title: Tool Surface Pinning

conformance_checks.buyer_question: Can SecurityRecipes block the realistic data-theft path created by composable tools?
conformance_checks.requirement: Sessions that combine private context, untrusted content, and external transmission must be held or killed before exfiltration.
conformance_checks.title: Private Data, Untrusted Content, and External Send

conformance_checks.buyer_question: How does a buyer know tool output cannot silently become untrusted prompt content?
conformance_checks.requirement: Tool outputs that return to the model should be schema-bound or tainted for downstream policy.

conformance_checks.requirement: Protocol conformance evidence should be exportable for procurement, platform review, and acquisition diligence.

buyer_views.question: Why is SecurityRecipes an acquirable secure context layer rather than a documentation library?
buyer_views.title: Acquisition Diligence

commercialization_path.acquirer_value: A model lab, AI platform vendor, or security company gets a standards-backed protocol control plane that sits directly between agents, tools, context, and remote-agent networks.
commercialization_path.enterprise_layer: Sell hosted MCP/A2A protocol drift monitoring, live metadata checks, signed tool-surface baselines, Agent Card verification, conformance APIs, and trust-center exports.
commercialization_path.open_layer: Publish protocol conformance as open evidence so teams can evaluate MCP, A2A, and agent boundary risk with a shared vocabulary.

buyer_success_criteria: Every protocol profile has current source references and generated evidence paths.
Every conformance check maps to at least one source pack that already exists in the repository.
Runtime evaluation is deterministic and fail-closed.
MCP and A2A are treated as complementary boundaries: MCP for tools and context, A2A for remote-agent delegation.
The open pack can be regenerated in CI and later extended into hosted protocol-drift monitoring.

control_checks.title: Trust-Center Protocol Export

enterprise_adoption_packet.board_level_claim: SecurityRecipes treats MCP and A2A as governable enterprise protocol surfaces with generated evidence and deterministic runtime decisions.
default_questions_answered: Which protocol versions and guidance are tracked?
Which generated packs prove MCP authorization, tool safety, A2A Agent Card trust, handoff, identity, and egress controls?
Which runtime fields must be present before protocol-mediated context or authority moves?
Which protocol drift conditions force hold, deny, or kill-session decisions?
How does the open knowledge layer become a hosted MCP/A2A conformance product?
enterprise_adoption_packet.recommended_first_use: Attach this pack to AI platform protocol review, MCP gateway intake, A2A pilot review, procurement security questionnaires, and acquisition diligence.
enterprise_adoption_packet.sales_motion: Lead with open protocol conformance evidence, then sell hosted live checks, drift alerts, signed receipts, fleet reporting, and private customer policy mapping.

intent: Create a buyer-grade protocol conformance layer for SecurityRecipes so MCP, A2A, agent identity, prompt-injection, and standards drift are translated into generated evidence, deterministic runtime decisions, and MCP-readable diligence artifacts.

positioning.buyer: AI platform, product security, AppSec, GRC, procurement security, and acquisition diligence teams
positioning.category: Agentic protocol conformance and drift evidence
positioning.enterprise_promise: SecurityRecipes can prove that fast-moving agent protocols are tracked, mapped to controls, regenerated in CI, and enforced before context, authority, tools, or remote-agent delegation crosses a trust boundary.
positioning.why_now: Agent systems are moving from single-agent pilots to protocol-mediated fleets. MCP and A2A make context, tools, and remote agents composable, but enterprise buyers need conformance gates for authorization metadata, resource binding, tool annotations, tool-surface drift, Agent Cards, authenticated extended cards, prompt-injection paths, and protocol-version drift.

protocol_profiles.title: Agent Identity, Protocol Handoff, and Prompt-Injection Boundary

source_references.name: Model Context Protocol Authorization Specification
source_references.why_it_matters: Protected MCP servers need OAuth 2.1 aligned authorization, protected-resource metadata, resource indicators, audience validation, PKCE, and token-passthrough denial before tools can safely receive agent authority.

source_references.name: MCP Authorization Client ID Metadata Document
source_references.why_it_matters: Client ID metadata gives MCP clients a scalable alternative to static registration, but enterprise gateways still need to review redirect policy, client identity, and authorization-server trust before approval.

source_references.name: Tool annotations in the Model Context Protocol
source_references.why_it_matters: MCP tool annotations can help clients present risk-aware approval UI, but annotations are hints until a trusted server, gateway policy, and drift baseline make them enforceable.

source_references.name: MCP Specification Changelog
source_references.why_it_matters: MCP is still changing quickly, so buyers need explicit evidence that resource metadata, authorization expectations, client metadata, and tool behavior are reviewed after spec revisions.

source_references.name: Agent2Agent Protocol Specification
source_references.why_it_matters: A2A Agent Cards, authenticated extended cards, transport security, version headers, skills, and security schemes create a remote-agent trust surface that should be evaluated before context or delegation moves.

source_references.name: AI Agent Standards Initiative
source_references.why_it_matters: NIST is prioritizing interoperable agent standards, agent authentication, identity infrastructure, and security evaluations for human-agent and multi-agent systems.

source_references.name: CAISI Request for Information on Securing AI Agent Systems
source_references.why_it_matters: CAISI highlights indirect prompt injection, poisoning, misaligned actions, and deployment access control as distinct agent-system security concerns.

source_references.name: OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications 2026
source_references.why_it_matters: OWASP gives buyers a shared language for agentic risks including tool misuse, identity abuse, context poisoning, insecure inter-agent communication, cascading failure, and rogue-agent behavior.

source_references.name: Designing AI agents to resist prompt injection
source_references.why_it_matters: OpenAI frames prompt injection as a source-to-sink and social-engineering problem, which requires data controls, confirmations, egress checks, and layered safeguards beyond string filtering.
